questions.text: Compound X & Y give effervesce with Na2CO3 solution, X gives a silver mirror with ammonical AgNO3 while Y gives sweet smell compound on heating with alcohol X & Y are
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Formic acid is known to produce a silver mirror with ammonical AgNO3 due to its aldehyde group. It also reacts with Na2CO3 to give effervescence. Acetic acid reacts with alcohol to form an ester, which has a sweet smell. Therefore, X is formic acid and Y is acetic acid.
While acetic acid does react with Na2CO3 and forms sweet-smelling esters with alcohol, it does not produce a silver mirror with ammonical AgNO3. Therefore, both compounds cannot be acetic acid.
Acetone does not react with Na2CO3 to produce effervescence. While formic acid does produce a silver mirror, acetone's lack of effervescence makes this pairing incorrect.
Acetone does not cause effervescence with Na2CO3. Since both compounds are supposed to produce effervescence, this option cannot be correct.

questions.explanation: Formic acid contains both a carboxyl group and an aldehyde group, which allows it to react with Na2CO3 to produce effervescence and with ammonical AgNO3 to produce a silver mirror. Acetic acid, on the other hand, forms sweet-smelling esters when heated with alcohol, which fits the description for compound Y. Thus, X must be formic acid and Y must be acetic acid.Options B, C, and D are incorrect because acetone does not produce effervescence with Na2CO3, and acetic acid does not produce a silver mirror with ammonical AgNO3.

questions.text: Acetic acid is soluble in?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acetic acid is infinitely miscible in water, alcohol, glycerol, ether, and carbon tetrachloride. It is practically insoluble in carbon disulfide. Aqueous solutions are stable at room temperature if well sealed.
HCI, HBr, and HI are all strong acids and can react with acetic acid to form salts and water. Therefore, they are not solvents for acetic acid.
Bromine water is not a suitable solvent for acetic acid as it is a nonpolar substance, and bromine water is a polar solvent.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This is an incorrect option.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect, as the acid that is isolated by some species of ants is called formic acid.
This option is correct, as the acid that is obtained from butter is called butyric acid.
This option is incorrect, as one of the main acids obtained from milk is lactic acid.
This option is correct, as acetic acid is typically isolated from vinegar, which is a dilute solution of acetic acid that is produced by the fermentation of ethanol by acetic acid bacteria.

questions.explanation: Acetic acid is typically isolated from vinegar, which is a dilute solution of acetic acid that is produced by the fermentation of ethanol by acetic acid bacteria.

questions.text: Maleic acid and fumaric acid are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is correct as when the substituent groups are oriented in the same direction, the diastereomer is referred to as cis, whereas, when the substituents are oriented in opposing directions, the diastereomer is referred to as trans. Maleic and fumaric acids are given below.
Option B is incorrect as chain isomers are compounds which have the same molecular formula but the carbon skeleton of the chain is different. Example: n-pentane and 2-methyl butane
Option C is incorrect as positional isomers are constitutional isomers with the same carbon skeleton and will have the same functional group. Example: 2-iodopentane and 3-iodopentane.
Option D is incorrect as metamers are the isomers having the same molecular formula but different alkyl groups on two sides of functional groups. Example: Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct as when the substituent groups are oriented in the same direction, the diastereomer is referred to as cis, whereas, when the substituents are oriented in opposing directions, the diastereomer is referred to as trans. Maleic and fumaric acids are given below. Option B is incorrect as chain isomers are compounds which have the same molecular formula but the carbon skeleton of the chain is different. questions.text: Acetic acid was first isolated from:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Ants: While some species of ants do produce formic acid as a defense mechanism, acetic acid is not typically associated with ants. The first isolation of acetic acid did not involve ants.
b) Butter: Butter is primarily composed of fats and does not contain significant amounts of acetic acid. The first isolation of acetic acid did not involve butter.
c) Milk: Milk contains various components, including proteins, fats, and lactose, but it does not naturally contain acetic acid. The first isolation of acetic acid did not involve milk.

d) Vinegar: This is the correct answer. Acetic acid was first isolated from vinegar. Vinegar is produced through the fermentation of ethanol by acetic acid bacteria. The fermentation process converts ethanol to acetic acid, leading to the sour taste and distinct aroma of vinegar.

questions.explanation: Acetic acid which is a dilute solution is used extensively as vinegar.

questions.text: The aliphatic monocarboxylic acids is obtained from:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Fats and oils: This is the correct answer. Aliphatic monocarboxylic acids are commonly obtained from fats and oils through a process called saponification or hydrolysis. Fats and oils are triglycerides, which are esters composed of glycerol and three fatty acid chains. When hydrolyzed, these fatty acid chains are released as aliphatic monocarboxylic acids.
b) Proteins and oils: While proteins contain amino acids with carboxyl groups, they are not a primary source of aliphatic monocarboxylic acids. Fats and oils are the primary sources.
c) Fats and proteins: While both fats and proteins contain carboxyl groups, aliphatic monocarboxylic acids are mainly obtained from fats.
d) Fats only: Fats are indeed a source of aliphatic monocarboxylic acids, but the correct answer includes both fats and oils.

questions.explanation: Fats and oils are hydrolyzed to form aliphatic monocarboxylic acids.

questions.text: Which of the following organic acid does NOT have a COOH group?

options.explanation: Acetic acid (CH3COOH)
Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, also known as vitamin C) has a COOH group, although it is not the typical carboxylic acid structure.
Butyric acid (CH3CH2CH2COOH)
Picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) does not have a COOH group. Instead, it has a phenol group (-OH) with three nitro groups (-NO2) attached to the benzene ring.

questions.explanation: Picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) does not have a COOH group. Instead, it has a phenol group (-OH) with three nitro groups (-NO2) attached to the benzene ring.

options.explanation: This is not a valid IUPAC term.

In the IUPAC system, carboxylic acids are also known as alkanoic acids. The suffix "-oic acid" is replaced with "-anoic acid" to indicate the presence of a carboxyl group (-COOH) in the molecule.For example:- Methanoic acid (HCOOH)- Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH)- Propanoic acid (CH₃CH₂COOH)
This term is used for unsaturated carboxylic acids, which have a double bond in the chain.
This term is used for esters, which are derivatives of carboxylic acids.

The suffix "-oic acid" is replaced with "-anoic acid" to indicate the presence of a carboxyl group (-COOH) in the molecule.For example:- Methanoic acid (HCOOH)- Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH)- Propanoic acid (CH₃CH₂COOH)

questions.text: 2-hydroxy propanoic acid is also called _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Stearic acid: Stearic acid is a long-chain saturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms. It is not the same as 2-hydroxy propanoic acid, which is a hydroxy acid with 3 carbon atoms.
Butyric acid: Butyric acid is a short-chain saturated fatty acid with 4 carbon atoms. It is not the same as 2-hydroxy propanoic acid, which has an additional hydroxyl group.
Maleic acid: Maleic acid is an unsaturated dicarboxylic acid with 4 carbon atoms. It is not the same as 2-hydroxy propanoic acid, which is a monoprotic hydroxy acid with 3 carbon atoms.
D is correct as common name for “2-hydroxy propanoic acid” is “Lactic Acid”

questions.explanation: Option D is correct as the common name of 2 hydroxy propanoic acid is "lactic acid" with a formula C3H6O3.

questions.text: Acetic acid is called _ acid.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Methanoic acid: Methanoic acid, also known as formic acid, has the chemical formula HCOOH. It is a simple carboxylic acid with one carbon atom.
b) Ethanoic acid: Ethanoic acid, also known as acetic acid, has the chemical formula CH3COOH. It is a carboxylic acid with two carbon atoms and is commonly found in vinegar.
c) Propanoic acid: Propanoic acid has the chemical formula CH3CH2COOH. It is a carboxylic acid with three carbon atoms.
d) Butanoic acid: Butanoic acid has the chemical formula CH3CH2CH2COOH. It is a carboxylic acid with four carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: Acetic acid (CH3COOH), also called ethanoic acid, the most important of the carboxylic acids. A dilute (approximately 5 percent by volume) solution of acetic acid produced by fermentation and oxidation of natural carbohydrates is called vinegar; a salt, ester, or acylal of acetic acid is called acetate.

questions.text: The structures of an acid and an alcohol are shown:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethanoic acid has two carbon atoms, but the acid in question has three, making this option incorrect.
Ethanoic acid has two carbon atoms, while the question specifies an acid with three carbon atoms. Additionally, propanol has three carbon atoms, but the alcohol in question has four.
Propanoic acid is correctly identified with three carbon atoms, but propanol has three carbon atoms, whereas the alcohol in question has four, making this option incorrect.
Propanoic acid is correctly identified with three carbon atoms, and butanol with four carbon atoms matches the description of the alcohol, making this the correct choice.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Acid=Propanoic, Alcohol=Butanol. The acid contains three carbon atoms, which corresponds to propanoic acid, as 'propane' indicates three carbons and '-oic acid' is the suffix for carboxylic acids. The alcohol contains four carbon atoms, which corresponds to butanol, with 'butane' indicating four carbons and '-ol' being the suffix for alcohols.Options A and B incorrectly identify the acid as ethanoic, which would have only two carbon atoms. Option C correctly identifies the acid but incorrectly identifies the alcohol as propanol, which has only three carbon atoms.

questions.text: In carboxylic acid molecule the bond angle between alkyl group and hydroxyl group is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct bond angle. A bond angle of 90° is characteristic of a molecule with sp3 hybridized atoms, but carboxylic acids have a different molecular geometry.
Option B is not the correct bond angle. A bond angle of 180° corresponds to a linear geometry, which is not applicable to carboxylic acids.
Option C is not the correct bond angle. A bond angle of 60° is characteristic of a molecule with sp2 hybridized atoms, but it does not apply to carboxylic acids.

Option D is the correct bond angle. The bond angle between the alkyl group and the hydroxyl group in a carboxylic acid is approximately 120°. Carboxylic acids have a planar geometry around the carbonyl carbon, which leads to a bond angle of approximately 120° between the alkyl group (R) and the hydroxyl group (OH). R | C=O | OH

questions.explanation: Sp2 hybridizationThe correct bond angle between the alkyl group and the hydroxyl group in a carboxylic acid molecule is D) 120°.

questions.text: Which of the following reagents helps us to distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct answer. Litmus solution is a pH indicator used to determine whether a substance is acidic or basic, but it cannot specifically distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid.

Option B is the correct answer. Tollen's reagent, also known as Fehling's solution B, is used to distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid. It is a mixture of copper sulfate (CuSO4) and potassium sodium tartrate (Rochelle salt). Methanoic acid (formic acid) reacts with Tollen's reagent to produce a brick-red precipitate of cuprous oxide (Cu2O). On the other hand, ethanoic acid (acetic acid) does not react with Tollen's reagent.

Option C is not the correct answer. Alcoholic silver nitrate (AgNO3) is used to distinguish between aliphatic and aromatic aldehydes or ketones, but it is not specific to distinguishing between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid.

Option D is not the correct answer. Sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) is used to test the presence of carboxylic acids. Both methanoic acid and ethanoic acid will react with sodium bicarbonate to produce carbon dioxide gas, but this test does not distinguish between the two acids.

questions.explanation: The correct reagent that helps distinguish between methanoic acid and ethanoic acid is B) Tollen's reagent (Fehling's solution B). Tollen's reagent reacts differently with each acid due to their unique chemical properties. Methanoic acid forms a brick-red precipitate of cuprous oxide with Tollen's reagent, while ethanoic acid does not react. This distinguishes between the two acids. The other options are incorrect as they are not specific reagents for distinguishing between these two acids.

Incorrect: Valeric acid ( C5H10O2 ). The formula clearly indicates 5 carbon atoms, not 7.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 5. Valeric acid has the chemical formula C5H10O2, which indicates that it contains 5 carbon (C) atoms. This is a straight-chain saturated fatty acid. The other options are incorrect because they suggest a different number of carbon atoms than what is present in Valeric acid's molecular structure.

questions.text: The irritation caused by red ants bite is due to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Lactic acid is produced in the muscles during intense exercise but is not involved in the irritation caused by insect bites. It does not have a role in the venomous or irritative properties of red ants.
Uric acid is a waste product formed from the breakdown of purines in the body. While it has various functions in metabolism, it is not responsible for the irritation caused by red ants.
Acetic acid is the main component of vinegar and has many uses, but it is not the acid responsible for the irritation from red ant bites. It does not have the same effects as formic acid.

Formic acid is the correct answer as it is the substance injected by red ants during a bite, causing irritation. This simple carboxylic acid is responsible for the painful and irritating effects associated with ant bites.

questions.explanation: The primary chemical responsible for the pain and irritation from many ant stings and bites is formic acid (methanoic acid). Ants inject this substance as a defense mechanism.

questions.text: Solubility of carboxylic acids decreases in water with increase in molar mass because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is not correct. The size or molar mass of a molecule is not directly related to its polarity. Polarity is determined by the presence of polar functional groups and the distribution of electron density in the molecule.

options.text: bigger molecules have bigger non-polar groups
options.explanation: The correct explanation for the decrease in solubility of carboxylic acids in water with an increase in molar mass is that the bigger molecules have bigger non-polar groups. As the molar mass increases, carboxylic acids tend to have larger hydrocarbon (non-polar) groups, which reduces their overall polarity and makes them less soluble in water.

options.text: bigger molecules make more hydrogen bonds
options.explanation: This statement is not correct. The number of hydrogen bonds formed by a molecule is not directly related to its molar mass. Hydrogen bonding is determined by the presence of hydrogen bond donor and acceptor groups in the molecule, such as -OH and -COOH groups in carboxylic acids.

options.text: bigger molecules can form lesser hydrogen bonds/molecule
options.explanation: This statement is not correct.Larger molecules can potentially form more hydrogen bonds, especially if they have multiple hydrogen bond donor and acceptor groups. However, this is not the primary reason for the decrease in solubility.

questions.explanation: The correct explanation for the decrease in solubility of carboxylic acids in water with an increase in molar mass is that the bigger molecules have bigger non-polar groups. As the molar mass increases, carboxylic acids tend to have larger hydrocarbon (non-polar) groups, which reduces their overall polarity and makes them less soluble in water.

questions.text: The correct statement among the following about HCOOH is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It is a stronger acid than CH3COOH
options.explanation: Formic acid (HCOOH) has a lower pKa value than acetic acid (CH3COOH), making it a stronger acid. This is due to the absence of an electron-donating methyl group, which increases the acidity of the carboxylic acid.
Formic acid can indeed reduce Tollen's reagent (ammoniacal silver nitrate) because it acts as a reducing agent, leading to the deposition of elemental silver, often seen as a silver mirror.

options.text: It gives CO and H2O on heating with conc.H2SO4
options.explanation: When heated with concentrated sulfuric acid, formic acid decomposes to produce carbon monoxide (CO) and water (H2O), illustrating its decomposition reaction.
All of the statements about formic acid (HCOOH) are correct. It is stronger than acetic acid, reduces Tollen's reagent, and decomposes to CO and water with concentrated sulfuric acid.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is All of the above. Formic acid (HCOOH) is indeed stronger than acetic acid (CH3COOH) due to its lower pKa value. It can reduce Tollen's reagent because it acts as a reducing agent. Additionally, when heated with concentrated sulfuric acid, formic acid decomposes to produce carbon monoxide and water. Each individual statement is true, thereby validating the option 'All of the above'.

questions.text: Why is chloroethanoic acid, ClCH2CO2H, a stronger acid than ethanoic acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Chlorine releases electrons and destabilizes ClCH2CO2- anion
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. HA---H++A-When A- formed is more stbale, the equilibrium lies more to the right. More HA dissociates and hence it is a stronger acid. The presence of Cl withdraws the negative charge by inductive effect since Cl is electronegative. This disperses the negative charge and hence stabilizes the anion.

The presence of Cl withdraws the negative charge by inductive effect since Cl is electronegative. This disperses the negative charge and hence stabilizes the anion.

The presence of Cl withdraws the negative charge by inductive effect since Cl is electronegative. This disperses the negative charge and hence stabilizes the anion.

The presence of Cl- withdraws the negative charge by inductive effect since Cl- is electronegative. This disperses the negative charge and hence stabilizes the anion.

questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic acids are known to be stronger acids than phenols, alcohols, and water due to the resonance stabilization of their conjugate base.
Phenol is more acidic than alcohols and water due to resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion, but it is not as acidic as carboxylic acids.
Water is less acidic than carboxylic acids and phenol because it lacks resonance stabilization of its conjugate base.
Alcohols are the least acidic among the given options due to the lack of significant resonance stabilization in their conjugate bases.

questions.explanation: Acid strength order:Carboxylic acid>Phenol>Alcohol≈Water

options.explanation: Carbolic acid is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 15. It is a slightly acidic compound and is slightly more acidic than water.

Methanol is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 15. It is a weaker acid than water and phenol.
Butanoic acid is a stronger organic acid with a pKa of around 4.5. It is a stronger acid than water, phenol, and methanol.

questions.text: Iodoform test is used to check the presence of :
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Amines: The Iodoform test is not typically used to detect amines. Amines do not generally react in a way that produces the characteristic yellow precipitate observed in the Iodoform test.

b) Carbonyl compounds: This is the correct answer. The Iodoform test is primarily used to detect the presence of carbonyl compounds, specifically those with a methyl ketone (RCOCH3) or a secondary alcohol (R2CHOH) functional group. When these compounds react with iodine and sodium hydroxide, they produce a yellow precipitate of iodoform (CHI3), which has a distinct odor.
c) Carboxylic acid: Carboxylic acids do not typically undergo the Iodoform test. The test is specific to compounds containing a methyl ketone or secondary alcohol group.
d) Alkyl halides: Alkyl halides do not usually give a positive Iodoform test. The test is specific to compounds with the appropriate carbonyl functional groups.

questions.explanation: The iodoform test is used to check the presence of carbonyl compounds with the structure R-CO-CH3 or alcohols with the structure R-CH(OH)-CH3 in a given unknown substance.

questions.text: Which is the strongest acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CH3COOH, or acetic acid, contains a methyl group (-CH3) which is an electron-donating group. This decreases acidity by increasing electron density on the carboxylate ion.
CH2ClCOOH, or monochloroacetic acid, has one chlorine atom, which is an electron-withdrawing group, making it more acidic than acetic acid but less than others with more chlorine atoms.
CHCl2COOH, or dichloroacetic acid, contains two chlorine atoms. These enhance acidity by withdrawing electrons, but it is not as strong as an acid with more chlorine atoms.
CCl3COOH, or trichloroacetic acid, contains three chlorine atoms, maximizing the electron-withdrawing effect. This significantly increases acidity, making it the strongest acid among the options.

questions.explanation: The acidity of a carboxylic acid is influenced by the presence of substituents that can stabilize the carboxylate ion. Electron-withdrawing groups, such as chlorine atoms, help stabilize the negative charge by dispersing it, thereby increasing acidity.In this question, CCl3COOH is the strongest acid because it has three chlorine atoms, which are highly effective electron-withdrawing groups. This makes the molecule more acidic than the others, which have fewer chlorine atoms or electron-donating groups like the methyl group in CH3COOH.

questions.text: Which one of the following is strongest acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) CH3COOH (Acetic acid):This is acetic acid, also known as ethanoic acid. It is a weak acid, and its acidity is derived from the dissociation of the hydrogen ion (H+) from the carboxyl group (–COOH). It partially dissociates in water to form H3O+ and CH3COO- ions. Acetic acid is commonly found in vinegar.

b) CH3CH2COOH (Propanoic acid):This is propanoic acid. It is also a weak acid, similar to acetic acid. Propanoic acid is derived from the dissociation of the hydrogen ion (H+) from the carboxyl group (–COOH). It partially dissociates in water to form H3O+ and CH3CH2COO- ions.

c) C6H5CG2COOH (Benzoic acid):This is benzoic acid, which contains a phenyl group (C6H5) attached to the carboxyl group (–COOH). Benzoic acid is a weak acid, but it is slightly stronger than acetic and propanoic acids due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the phenyl group, which stabilizes the resulting carboxylate ion after dissociation.

d) FCH2COOH (Fluoroacetic acid):This is fluoroacetic acid, which has a fluorine atom attached to the carboxyl group (–COOH). Fluoroacetic acid is the strongest acid among the given options. The presence of the fluorine atom significantly increases the acidity of the carboxyl group, making it a strong acid. Fluoroacetic acid is highly toxic and not naturally occurring.

questions.explanation: D is the correct option, as it contains a halogen which have strong electron-withdrawing effects.

questions.text: Acetic acid is a weak acid because in aqueous solution it ionizes:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While acetic acid does ionize partially, this option is misleading because 'partially soluble' refers to solubility, not ionization. Acetic acid is actually highly soluble in water but only partially ionizes.
If an acid ionizes completely, it is considered strong. Acetic acid is a weak acid, meaning it cannot ionize completely in water.
Acetic acid does not ionize easily because it is a weak acid. 'Easily' would suggest it ionizes more readily, which is incorrect.
Acetic acid ionizes with difficulty in water, meaning it does not release many hydrogen ions, which is characteristic of a weak acid.
This option is invalid because the correct answer is already provided.

questions.explanation: Weak acids are acids that don't completely dissociate in solution. The strength of a weak acid depends on how much it dissociates: the more it dissociates, the stronger the acid.The strength of an acid depends upon the extent of ionization. Acetic acid is highly soluble in water but it dissociates partially in the aqueous solution to produce a small amount of H+ ions and therefore. considered as a weak acid.Option A: Acetic acid ionizes to a very little extent in water,so partially soluble is not the right answer.Option B: An acid which is completely ionized in its aqueous solution is considered a strong acid.Since,acetic acid is a weak acid,it can not ionize completely in water.Option C: Acetic acid is a weak acid and hence,can not ionize easily in a solution.Option D: Since acetic acid is not a strong enough proton donor to be entirely converted to hydronium ions in aqueous solution, it is called a weak acid.Hence,acetic acid is considered a weak acid because of its difficult ionization Option E: Since we have already found our option,this option is not valid.

questions.text: All of the following methods are used to prepare carboxylic acids EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: By the oxidation of primary alcohol
options.explanation: Alcohols can be oxidized to carboxylic acids using strong oxidizing agents like potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or chromic acid (H2CrO4). The oxidation reaction is represented as:R-OH + O -> R-COOH

options.text: By acid hydrolysis of alkane nitrite
options.explanation: The acid hydrolysis of alkane nitrites can yield carboxylic acids, as shown in the reaction:R-ONO2 + H2O -> R-COOH + HNO2However, this method is rarely used due to the instability of alkane nitrites.

options.text: By the reaction R-Mg-Br with CO2 followed by acid hydrolysis
options.explanation: Grignard reagents, formed from alkyl halides and magnesium, react with carbon dioxide to produce carboxylic acids. The reaction is:R-Mg-Br + CO2 -> R-COOH + MgBr2
Aldehydes cannot be converted to carboxylic acids through reduction; they are already a different functional group. The reduction reaction is:R-CHO + H2 -> R-COOH

questions.explanation: The reduction of an aldehyde, typically using a reducing agent like NaBH4 or LiAIH4 yields a primary alcohol. The other three options are standard methods for synthesizing carboxylic acids.

questions.text: By oxidation of which compound carboxylic acid cannot be obtained?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-Methyl-2-propanol is a tertiary alcohol. So it cannot be oxidized to carboxylic acid because it does not contain α-H. Under same conditions it show elimination and give alkene.
1-butanol is a primary alcohol, that gives carboxylic acid on oxidation by almost every oxidizer.
2-butanol is a secondary alcohol that gives ketones on oxidation. Ketones can be further oxidized to give carboxylic acids.It needs strong oxidizers.
2-Propanol is a secondary alcohol that gives ketones on oxidation. Ketones can be further oxidized to give carboxylic acids.It needs strong oxidizers.

questions.explanation: 2-Methyl-2-propanol: This is a tertiary alcohol (3° alcohol), as the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group (OH) is attached to three other carbon atoms. Tertiary alcohols cannot be oxidized to form carboxylic acids directly. The oxidation of tertiary alcohols results in the formation of ketones, not carboxylic acids.

questions.text: A compound is oxidized to X, by further oxidation of X if resulting compound is butyric acid, starting compound can be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Butanol can be oxidized to butanal, which upon further oxidation forms butanoic acid (butyric acid). This involves an oxidation of the primary alcohol group to an aldehyde and then to a carboxylic acid.
Butanoic acid is the same as butyric acid, so it cannot be the starting compound that is oxidized to butyric acid.
Butanone is a ketone and cannot be directly oxidized to a carboxylic acid with typical oxidizing agents because it lacks the necessary hydrogen atom on the carbonyl carbon.
Only butanol can be oxidized to butanoic acid (butyric acid), making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: Butanol is the correct starting compound because it is a primary alcohol. Primary alcohols can be oxidized first to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids. In this case, butanol is oxidized to butanal and then further oxidized to butanoic acid (butyric acid). Butanoic acid is already the final product, so it cannot be the starting compound. Butanone is a ketone and cannot undergo oxidation to a carboxylic acid under typical conditions because ketones do not have the necessary hydrogen atom for such a transformation. The option All of these is incorrect because only butanol can follow the oxidation pathway needed to produce butanoic acid from a different starting compound.

questions.text: 1 mole of X, where X is an organic compound requires 0.5 moles of oxygen to produce carboxylic acid, the compound may be
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohols typically require more than 0.5 moles of oxygen for complete oxidation to carboxylic acids via aldehydes, making this option incorrect.
Ketones do not directly oxidize to carboxylic acids under mild conditions; they require breaking of carbon-carbon bonds, hence this option is incorrect.
Ethers have different oxidation pathways and do not directly form carboxylic acids, thus requiring different conditions, so this option is incorrect.
Aldehydes readily oxidize to carboxylic acids with 0.5 moles of oxygen per mole of aldehyde, making this option correct.

questions.explanation: Aldehydes are easily oxidized to carboxylic acids with 0.5 moles of oxygen per mole of aldehyde, consistent with the reaction conditions provided in the question. Alcohols require more oxygen as they first oxidize to aldehydes, then to carboxylic acids. Ketones do not directly oxidize to carboxylic acids without breaking carbon-carbon bonds, and ethers have different oxidation requirements altogether, making these options incorrect.

questions.text: Carboxylic acids are produced by the oxidation of _?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: All of the above can oxidise and form carboxylic acids.

questions.text: Arrange the following in increasing strength of carboxylic acid with respect to chloro-substituted acids?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that trichloroacetic acid is the weakest, which contradicts the effect of electron-withdrawing chlorine atoms.
This option incorrectly places acetic acid, which has no chlorine atoms, as stronger than chloroacetic acids.
This option incorrectly suggests monochloroacetic acid is weaker than acetic acid and misplaces the position of dichloroacetic acid.
This is the correct order, as the acidity increases with the number of electron-withdrawing chlorine atoms.

questions.explanation: The acidity of carboxylic acids is influenced by the presence of electron-withdrawing groups, such as chlorine atoms. The more chlorine atoms present, the stronger the acid. This is because chlorine is highly electronegative, and it stabilizes the negative charge on the carboxylate ion through inductive effects, making the acid stronger. Therefore, the correct order from weakest to strongest acid is: CH3COOH (acetic acid, no Cl) < CH2ClCOOH (monochloroacetic acid, one Cl) < CHCl2COOH (dichloroacetic acid, two Cl) < CCl3COOH (trichloroacetic acid, three Cl). Other options are incorrect because they do not follow this trend.

questions.text: Carboxylic acid is more acidic than phenol because of the greater stability of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests that carboxylic acid itself is more stable, leading to its higher acidity compared to phenol. While carboxylic acids are indeed more acidic than phenols, the stability of the acid itself is not the primary reason for this difference in acidity.

Option B refers to the stability of the proton (H+) that is released during the deprotonation of a carboxylic acid or phenol. While the stability of the proton does play a role in the overall acidity, it is not the main factor that contributes to the difference in acidity between carboxylic acids and phenols.

Option C correctly identifies the reason for the higher acidity of carboxylic acids. When a carboxylic acid (RCOOH) loses a proton, it forms a carboxylate ion (RCOO-) and releases a hydrogen ion (H+). The carboxylate ion (RCOO-) is stabilized by resonance, which distributes the negative charge over two oxygen atoms. This resonance stabilization makes the carboxylate ion more stable compared to the phenoxide ion formed from phenols.

Option D correctly identifies the reason for the higher acidity of carboxylic acids. When a carboxylic acid (RCOOH) loses a proton, it forms a carboxylate ion (RCOO-) and releases a hydrogen ion (H+). The carboxylate ion (RCOO-) is stabilized by resonance, which distributes the negative charge over two oxygen atoms. This resonance stabilization makes the carboxylate ion more stable compared to the phenoxide ion formed from phenols.

questions.explanation: Option D correctly identifies the reason for the higher acidity of carboxylic acids. When a carboxylic acid (RCOOH) loses a proton, it forms a carboxylate ion (RCOO-) and releases a hydrogen ion (H+). The carboxylate ion (RCOO-) is stabilized by resonance, which distributes the negative charge over two oxygen atoms. This resonance stabilization makes the carboxylate ion more stable compared to the phenoxide ion formed from phenols.

questions.text: Among acetic acid, phenol and n-hexanol, which one of the following compounds will react with NaHCO3 solution to give sodium salt and CO₂?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acetic acid (CH3COOH) is a weak acid. When it reacts with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) solution, it undergoes a neutralization reaction. The bicarbonate ion (HCO3-) acts as a base and reacts with the acidic proton of acetic acid, resulting in the formation of sodium acetate (CH3COONa) salt and carbon dioxide (CO2) gas.

n-hexanol (C6H14O) is an alcohol and does not contain a carboxylic acid functional group. Therefore, it does not undergo a reaction with sodium bicarbonate solution to produce a sodium salt and carbon dioxide.

As explained earlier, acetic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate to form sodium acetate and carbon dioxide. Phenol (C6H6O), on the other hand, is an aromatic compound and does not contain a carboxylic acid functional group. Therefore, it does not undergo a reaction with sodium bicarbonate to produce a sodium salt and carbon dioxide.
Phenol, as mentioned above, does not contain a carboxylic acid functional group. Hence, it does not react with sodium bicarbonate solution to give sodium salt and carbon dioxide.

questions.explanation: Acetic acid (CH3COOH) is a weak acid. When it reacts with sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) solution, it undergoes a neutralization reaction. The bicarbonate ion (HCO3-) acts as a base and reacts with the acidic proton of acetic acid, resulting in the formation of sodium acetate (CH3COONa) salt and carbon dioxide (CO2) gas.

questions.text: Which of the following is the functional group for carboxylic acids?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The following group is a carbonyl group (C=O).
The following functional group is an aldehyde group.
The following group is a hydroxyl grop.

The carboxyl functional group that characterizes the carboxylic acids is unusual in that it is composed of two functional groups: (1) the carboxyl group and (2) a hydroxyl group bonded to a carbonyl group. It is often written in condensed form as –CO2H or –COOH.

questions.explanation: The carboxyl functional group that characterizes the carboxylic acids is unusual in that it is composed of two functional groups: (1) the carboxyl group and (2) a hydroxyl group bonded to a carbonyl group. It is often written in condensed form as –CO2H or –COOH.

questions.text: The protonation of carboxylic acid is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Explanation:It is the fact. Equilibrium constant is products/reactants. Hence the equilibrium constant Kc forH2O → H+ + OH-can be written as follows:Kc = [products] /[reactants]Kc = [H+] + [OH-] / [H2O]

ANSWER: BExplanation:It is the fact. Equilibrium constant is products/reactants. Hence the equilibrium constant Kc forH2O → H+ + OH-can be written as follows:Kc = [products] /[reactants]Kc = [H+] + [OH-] / [H2O]

Explanation:It is the fact. Equilibrium constant is products/reactants. Hence the equilibrium constant Kc forH2O → H+ + OH-can be written as follows:Kc = [products] /[reactants]Kc = [H+] + [OH-] / [H2O]

Explanation:It is the fact. Equilibrium constant is products/reactants. Hence the equilibrium constant Kc forH2O → H+ + OH-can be written as follows:Kc = [products] /[reactants]Kc = [H+] + [OH-] / [H2O]

questions.explanation: Explanation:During protonation, the double bond between the carbon and oxygen is broken. As a result, a long pair is present on the oxygen atom as a result hydrogen ion can form the bond with it. Thus the correct option is B.

questions.text: What is the general formula of Ester?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) R-COO-R: This is the correct general formula for an ester. Esters are organic compounds formed by the reaction between a carboxylic acid (R-COOH) and an alcohol (R-OH). The general structure of an ester is R-COO-R', where R and R' represent alkyl or aryl groups.

b) R-CHO: This is not the general formula of an ester. R-CHO represents an aldehyde, which is a different type of organic compound. Aldehydes have a carbonyl group (C=O) with one hydrogen atom and one alkyl or aryl group attached.

c) -OH: This represents the hydroxyl functional group, which is present in alcohols and other compounds, but it is not the general formula of an ester. Esters have a carbonyl group (C=O) and an alkoxy group (R-O) in their structure.
d) RX: This represents an alkyl halide, where X is a halogen atom (e.g., chlorine, bromine, iodine). Alkyl halides are a different class of organic compounds and are not the same as esters.

questions.explanation: a) R-COO-R: This is the correct general formula for an ester. Esters are organic compounds formed by the reaction between a carboxylic acid (R-COOH) and an alcohol (R-OH). The general structure of an ester is R-COO-R', where R and R' represent alkyl or aryl groups.

questions.text: Which sequence of groups replace OH of carboxylic acid to halides, esters and amides respectively:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option lists the groups in the order of the original functional group and its replacements. However, it does not correctly represent the sequence of nucleophilic substitutions that occur during the formation of carboxylic acid derivatives.

This is the correct answer. In carboxylic acids, the hydroxyl (-OH) group can be replaced in a specific sequence: first by a halide (-X) to form acid halides, then by an alkoxy group (-OR) to create esters, and finally by an amine (-NH2) to yield amides.
This option does not accurately reflect the sequence of group replacements in carboxylic acids. The order presented here is incorrect, as it does not follow the nucleophilic substitution pattern.

This option incorrectly includes a nitro group (-NO2), which is not a product of the replacement of the -OH group in carboxylic acids. The correct derivatives are halides, esters, and amides, not nitro compounds.

questions.explanation: Carboxylic acid derivatives are formed by replacing the -OH group. Replacing it with a halogen (-X) gives an acid halide. Replacing it with an alkoxy group (-OR) gives an ester. Replacing it with an amino group (-NH2) gives an amide.

questions.text: For producing a carboxylic acid derivative the reaction of nucleophile to carboxyl group is always followed by displacement of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: For Nucleophilic substitution reaction of carboxylic acid →C-O bond breaks →-OH replace by -OR, -X, RCOO- and NH2

Reactions Involving the-OH Group of Carboxylic Acids: The addition of a nucleophile to the carboxyl group is always followed by the displacement of the -OH group by some other group, producing a carboxylic acid derivative.

Reactions Involving the-OH Group of Carboxylic Acids: The addition of a nucleophile to the carboxyl group is always followed by the displacement of the OH group by some other group, producing a carboxylic acid derivative. If only H+ atom displaces then carboxylic acid derivates cannot be formed.
There is no such group present in carboxylic acid.

questions.explanation: For Nucleophilic substitution reaction of carboxylic acid →C-O bond breaks →-OH replace by -OR, -X, RCOO- and NH2

questions.text: An organic acid Z reacts separately with sodium bicarbonate,sodium hydroxide and sodium carbonate.Which one of the following represent the structure of Z?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethyl formate does contain a carbonyl group (C=O), which is typically found in carboxylic acids. However, it lacks the carboxyl group (-COOH) that defines carboxylic acids. When ethyl formate reacts with sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, or sodium carbonate, it would not yield the characteristic reactions of carboxylic acids with these compounds.

Ethanol is an alcohol, not a carboxylic acid. It does not have the carboxyl group (-COOH) that is essential for organic acids. Therefore, it would not exhibit the typical reactions of carboxylic acids with sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, or sodium carbonate.

Propene is an alkene, not a carboxylic acid. It does not contain the carboxyl group (-COOH) characteristic of organic acids. As a result, it would not undergo the reactions that carboxylic acids exhibit with sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, or sodium carbonate.

Propanoic acid is a carboxylic acid with the structural formula H3C-CH2-COOH.It contains the carboxyl group (-COOH), which is responsible for the acidic properties of carboxylic acids. When Propanoic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, or sodium carbonate in the following way:With sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), it forms sodium acetate,carbon dioxide, and water.With sodium hydroxide (NaOH), it forms sodium acetate and water.With sodium carbonate (Na2CO3),it forms sodium acetate,carbon dioxide, and water. These reactions are characteristic of carboxylic acids.

questions.explanation: Propanoic acid is a carboxylic acid with the structural formula H3C-CH2-COOH.It contains the carboxyl group (-COOH), which is responsible for the acidic properties of carboxylic acids. When Propanoic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, or sodium carbonate in the following way: With sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), it forms sodium acetate,carbon dioxide, and water.With sodium hydroxide (NaOH), it forms sodium acetate and water.With sodium carbonate (Na2CO3),it forms sodium acetate,carbon dioxide, and water. These reactions are characteristic of carboxylic acids.
hints: sodium bicarbonate,sodium hydroxide and sodium carbonate

questions.text: Which chemical test is used to distinguish between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Tollen's test is not specifically used to distinguish between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate. It is a test used to detect the presence of aldehydes, which react with Tollen's reagent (ammoniacal silver nitrate) to form a silver mirror. This test is not applicable to differentiating between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate.

Fehling's test is also not suitable for distinguishing between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate. It is a test used to detect the presence of reducing sugars, such as aldehydes and certain ketones. It involves the reaction of Fehling's reagent with the reducing sugar to form a reddish-brown precipitate of copper(I) oxide. This test does not provide specific information about benzoic acid or ethyl benzoate.

The iodoform test is not effective in differentiating between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate. It is primarily used to detect the presence of methyl ketones that have a methyl group adjacent to a carbonyl group. Benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate do not possess the necessary structural features to give a positive iodoform test.

The sodium bicarbonate test is the correct option. It is a chemical test used to distinguish between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate. Benzoic acid, being a carboxylic acid, reacts with sodium bicarbonate solution to produce carbon dioxide gas (effervescence) and a corresponding sodium salt. Ethyl benzoate, an ester, does not undergo this reaction because esters do not have acidic hydrogens available for the reaction with bicarbonate ions. Therefore, the absence or presence of effervescence when treated with sodium bicarbonate solution can differentiate between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate.

questions.explanation: The sodium bicarbonate test is the correct option. It is a chemical test used to distinguish between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate. Benzoic acid, being a carboxylic acid, reacts with sodium bicarbonate solution to produce carbon dioxide gas (effervescence) and a corresponding sodium salt. Ethyl benzoate, an ester, does not undergo this reaction because esters do not have acidic hydrogens available for the reaction with bicarbonate ions. Therefore, the absence or presence of effervescence when treated with sodium bicarbonate solution can differentiate between benzoic acid and ethyl benzoate.

questions.text: By treating CO2 with Grignards's reagent, followed by acid hydrolysis which compound is obtained?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aromatic compounds cannot be formed by treating CO2 with Grignard's reagent. The reaction specifically leads to the formation of carboxylic acids, not aromatic structures.
Alcohols are not the direct product of the reaction between CO2 and Grignard's reagent. The correct product after hydrolysis is a carboxylic acid, not an alcohol.
Carboxylic acids are indeed produced by treating CO2 with Grignard's reagent followed by acid hydrolysis. This is a well-known reaction in organic chemistry.
Ethers cannot be produced from the reaction of CO2 and Grignard's reagent. The reaction specifically leads to the formation of carboxylic acids upon hydrolysis.

questions.explanation: The Grignard reagent acts as a strong nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbon of carbon dioxide. The resulting magnesium carboxylate salt, upon protonation with a dilute acid (acid hydrolysis), yields a carboxylic acid..

questions.text: NucleophiIic reactions of carboxylic acids are due to _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect because the –OH group in carboxylic acids does not make the molecule electrophilic enough for nucleophilic attack. Instead, it mainly affects acidic properties rather than facilitating nucleophilic reactions.

This is also incorrect. The C–O single bond is a sigma bond, and it is not sufficiently electrophilic or reactive to attract nucleophiles. It is relatively stable and not the site of attack in typical nucleophilic reactions.

This is the correct answer. The carbonyl group (C=O) is highly electrophilic because of the partial positive charge on the carbon atom, which attracts nucleophiles. Most nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions occur due to the presence of this carbonyl carbon.
This is incorrect because Option C is correct. The nucleophilic reactivity of carboxylic acids can be directly attributed to the C=O group.

questions.explanation: In general, carboxylic acids undergo a nucleophilic substitution reaction where the nucleophile (-OH) is substituted by another nucleophile (Nu). The carbonyl group (C=O) gets polarized (i.e. there is a charge separation), since oxygen is more electronegative than carbon and pulls the electron density towards itself.Only option C is correct

questions.text: The product for the given reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Structure: Cyclohexene with a =CH₂ group (i.e., methylene cyclohexane).Explanation:This is the correct product.The –CH₂OH group loses water and forms a double bond at the exocyclic position.This is called an exocyclic alkene (double bond between ring carbon and exo carbon).Matches expected product of alcohol dehydration

Structure: Cyclohexane (saturated six-membered ring).Explanation:This is fully saturated and has no functional group.Does not match the product of acid-catalyzed alcohol dehydration (which should be an alkene).❌ Incorrect. No elimination took place.

Structure: Cyclohexene (a double bond inside the ring).Explanation:A double bond within the ring suggests a rearranged product.However, in this case, dehydration of –CH₂OH on cyclohexyl does not lead to internal cyclohexene.There's no hydride shift possible to move the carbocation into the ring.❌ Incorrect. Wrong position of double bond.

Does not follow reaction mechanism.

questions.explanation: Structure: Cyclohexene with a =CH₂ group (i.e., methylene cyclohexane).Explanation:This is the correct product.The –CH₂OH group loses water and forms a double bond at the exocyclic position.This is called an exocyclic alkene (double bond between ring carbon and exo carbon).Matches expected product of alcohol dehydration

questions.text: Which of the following causes complete reduction of a carboxylic acid into alkanes?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Although the hydrogenation of an alkene is a thermodynamically favourable reaction, it will not proceed without the addition of a catalyst. Common catalysts used are insoluble metals such as palladium in the form Pd-C, platinum in the form PtO2, and nickel in the form Ra-Ni.

Palladium on activated carbon (Pd/C) is extensively used as a heterogeneous catalyst for hydrogenation in synthetic organic chemistry because of its high catalyst activity, cost-efficiency, easy separation from the reaction mixture, and reusability such as alkyl halide into alkane.
"HI" reduces carboxylic acid to alkanes in the presence of "P" as a catalyst as it is a strong reducing agent.

The aldehydes or ketones are reduced by LiAlH4 to the corresponding primary or secondary alcohols respectively. E.g. Acetaldehyde is reduced to ethyl alcohol and acetone is reduced to isopropyl alcohol.

questions.explanation: "HI" reduces carboxylic acid to alkanes in the presence of "P" as a catalyst as it is a strong reducing agent.

questions.text: Which acid is used in the manufacture of plastics?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbolic acid, or phenol, is primarily used in the production of resins and adhesives, not as a primary component in the manufacture of plastics.
Acetic acid is used in the production of cellulose acetate, a material used in making synthetic fibers and plastics. It serves as a building block for these substances.
Carbonic acid is not typically used in the production of plastics. It is more commonly associated with carbonation processes in beverages.
Oxalic acid is not used as a monomer in plastic manufacture; it is more often used in cleaning and bleaching applications.

questions.explanation: Explanation:Acetic acid is used in the manufacture of plastics such as poly vinyl acetate (rayon) and cellulose acetate (silk).Carbolic acid, also known as phenol, is not used in the manufacture of plastics.Carbonic acid is not used in the manufacture of plastics.Oxalic acid is also not commonly used as a monomer in the manufacture of plastics.
